1:
"Therefore seeing we have this ministry, as we have received mercy, we faint not;" The witnesses note Paul's perseverance in the ministry received by mercy. On "seeing we have this ministry," the grammatical voices note the glorious office. Barnes and Robertson note Paul refers back to the glorious ministry of the new covenant just described (ch. 3); "having this ministry" — so high and honourable a trust — is the ground of his perseverance.

On "as we have received mercy," the witnesses note the humility. Barnes and Chrysostom note Paul ascribes his having this ministry to God's mercy — not to his own merit; he, the former persecutor, was made a minister by sheer grace (1 Timothy 1:13).

On "we faint not," the puritan Henry and Reformed Calvin note the perseverance: because the ministry is so glorious and was given by mercy, Paul does not "faint" (lose heart, grow weary, give up) amid its trials. The verse displays Paul's perseverance in the ministry received by mercy.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul refers to the glorious ministry of the new covenant (ch. 3); "having this ministry," so high a trust, grounds his perseverance (Barnes, Robertson); he ascribes it to God's mercy, not his merit — the former persecutor made a minister by grace (1 Timothy 1:13, Barnes, Chrysostom); and so "we faint not," he does not lose heart amid trials (Henry, Calvin). The verse displays Paul's perseverance. The verse marks the ministry: this glorious trust. The verse displays the source: received mercy. The verse displays the humility: not merit. The verse displays the resolve: we faint not. The perseverance displays the glorious ministry sustaining Paul amid trials. The verse displays the ministry: glorious. The verse displays the mercy: its source. The verse displays the steadfastness: not fainting. Paul's perseverance in the ministry received by mercy displays the ground of his steadfastness: because so glorious a ministry was entrusted to him by God's sheer mercy, the former persecutor does not faint or lose heart amid its trials. The verse displays the apostle's endurance grounded in grace, expressed in his renunciation of all deceit (v.2).
2:
"But have renounced the hidden things of dishonesty, not walking in craftiness, nor handling the word of God deceitfully; but by manifestation of the truth commending ourselves to every man's conscience in the sight of God." The witnesses note Paul's renunciation of all deceit and his open dealing with the truth. On "renounced the hidden things of dishonesty," the grammatical voices note the disavowal. Barnes and Robertson note Paul has "renounced" (cast off) "the hidden things of dishonesty (shame)" — secret, shameful practices; he uses no concealed, underhanded methods, unlike the false teachers.

On "not walking in craftiness, nor handling the word of God deceitfully," the witnesses note the integrity. Barnes and Calvin note Paul does not act with "craftiness" (cunning, trickery) nor "handle the word of God deceitfully" (adulterate, falsify, corrupt the Scriptures for gain or to please men) — he keeps the gospel pure.

On "by manifestation of the truth commending ourselves to every man's conscience in the sight of God," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the method: Paul commends himself simply by the open manifestation of the truth, appealing to every man's conscience, and acting always "in the sight of God." The verse displays Paul's renunciation of deceit and open dealing with the truth.

The witnesses agree: Paul has "renounced the hidden things of dishonesty (shame)" — secret, shameful practices, using no concealed methods (Barnes, Robertson); he does not walk in "craftiness" nor "handle the word of God deceitfully" (adulterate or corrupt the Scriptures for gain or to please men), keeping the gospel pure (Barnes, Calvin); and commends himself simply by the open "manifestation of the truth," appealing to every man's conscience, acting "in the sight of God" (Wesley). The verse displays Paul's open dealing. The verse marks the renunciation: hidden things of shame. The verse displays the integrity: no craft, no deceit. The verse displays the method: manifestation of the truth. The verse displays the appeal: to conscience, before God. The renunciation displays Paul's transparent, honest handling of the gospel. The verse displays the disavowal: of deceit. The verse displays the purity: of the word. The verse displays the openness: before God. Paul's renunciation of all deceit and his open dealing with the truth display the integrity of his ministry: he casts off secret and shameful methods, uses no craft, and does not corrupt the word of God, but commends himself simply by the plain manifestation of the truth to every conscience, in the sight of God. The verse displays the apostle's transparent honesty, set against the blindness of those who reject the gospel (vv.3-4).
3:
"But if our gospel be hid, it is hid to them that are lost:" The brief verse notes that the gospel is hidden only to the lost. The grammatical voices note the qualification. Barnes and Robertson note Paul answers an objection: if his gospel (so clear and open, v.2) is "hid" (veiled, obscure) to some, it is hid only "to them that are lost" (the perishing, those on the way to ruin) — not from any defect in the gospel.

On "them that are lost," the witnesses note the cause. Barnes notes the fault lies with the hearers, not the message: the gospel is plain, but the lost cannot see it because of their own perishing condition; the obscurity is in them, not in it.

The Reformed Calvin notes the point: the clarity of the gospel is not diminished by the blindness of those who reject it; if any do not see it, it is because they are perishing. The verse displays that the gospel is hidden only to the lost.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul answers an objection — if his clear gospel is "hid" to some, it is hid only "to them that are lost" (the perishing), not from any defect in the gospel (Barnes, Robertson); the fault lying with the hearers, the lost unable to see the plain message because of their own perishing condition (Barnes). Calvin notes the gospel's clarity is not diminished by the blindness of those who reject it. The verse displays the gospel hidden only to the lost. The verse marks the qualification: if hid. The verse displays the limit: to the lost. The verse displays the cause: in the hearers. The verse displays the clarity: of the gospel. The qualification displays the obscurity lying in the perishing, not the gospel. The verse displays the hiding: to the lost. The verse displays the fault: in them. The verse displays the gospel: plain. That the gospel is hidden only to the lost displays the true location of the obscurity: the gospel is plain and open, and if it is veiled to any, it is only to the perishing, whose own condition blinds them — the fault in the hearer, not the message. The verse displays the blindness of the lost, traced to the god of this world (v.4).
4:
"In whom the god of this world hath blinded the minds of them which believe not, lest the light of the glorious gospel of Christ, who is the image of God, should shine unto them." The witnesses note the blinding of unbelievers' minds — the god of this world crux. On "the god of this world hath blinded the minds," the great debate divides. The grammatical-historical Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott, and the Reformed Calvin take "the god of this world" as Satan — so called not by essence but because the wicked world worships and obeys him as its god (the prince of this world, John 12:31; Ephesians 2:2); he blinds the minds of unbelievers. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke (with the Fathers — Irenaeus, Tertullian, Chrysostom, Theodoret, Augustine) prefers to read it of the true God, who judicially blinds the unbelievers of this world (Isaiah 6:9; Romans 11:8), re-parsing "of this world" with "unbelievers."

On "the light of the glorious gospel of Christ, who is the image of God," the witnesses note the deity of Christ. Barnes and Cyril note Christ is "the image of God" — the exact representation of God's nature and glory (Colossians 1:15; Hebrews 1:3) — and the gospel reveals his glory; Satan blinds men lest this light shine on them.

The witnesses note the design: the blinding is to keep the saving light of the gospel from the lost. The verse displays the blinding of unbelievers' minds, with the god of this world crux.

The witnesses agree the design — the minds of unbelievers are blinded lest the light of the gospel of Christ (who is "the image of God," exactly representing his nature and glory, Colossians 1:15) shine on them (Barnes, Cyril). But "the god of this world" divides them: Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott, and Calvin take it as Satan — so called not by essence but because the wicked world worships and obeys him (John 12:31; Ephesians 2:2); Clarke, with the Fathers (Chrysostom, Augustine), prefers the true God, who judicially blinds the unbelievers of this world (Isaiah 6:9; Romans 11:8), re-parsing "of this world" with "unbelievers." The verse displays the blinding of unbelievers. The verse marks the blinding: of their minds. The verse displays the debate: Satan or God. The verse displays the deity: Christ the image of God. The verse displays the design: keeping out the light. The crux displays the blinding agent disputed, the gospel light withheld. The verse displays the blinding: of unbelievers. The verse displays the gospel: the light. The verse displays the Christ: the image of God. The blinding of unbelievers' minds, with the god of this world crux, displays the dark cause of unbelief: the minds of the perishing are blinded lest the light of the glorious gospel of Christ — the image of God — shine on them; the blinding agent read by most as Satan (the god this world worships) and by Clarke and the Fathers as God judicially blinding the obstinate. The verse displays the deep cause of spiritual blindness, answered by the open preaching of Christ (v.5).
5:
"For we preach not ourselves, but Christ Jesus the Lord; and ourselves your servants for Jesus' sake." The witnesses note that Paul preaches Christ as Lord, and himself as servant. On "we preach not ourselves, but Christ Jesus the Lord," the grammatical voices note the subject. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's central proclamation: he does not preach "ourselves" (his own glory, opinions, or party) but "Christ Jesus the Lord" — Christ as the supreme Lord and Saviour, the sole subject of his preaching.

On "ourselves your servants for Jesus' sake," the witnesses note the apostolic humility. Barnes and Calvin note that Paul presents himself not as lord over their faith but as their "servant (bondslave) for Jesus' sake" — devoting himself to their good for Christ's sake; the minister serves the church under Christ.

On "Christ Jesus the Lord," the eastern Cyril notes the confession of Christ's lordship — the deity and sovereignty of Christ proclaimed. The verse displays that Paul preaches Christ as Lord, and himself as servant.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul does not preach "ourselves" (his own glory or party) but "Christ Jesus the Lord" — the supreme Lord and Saviour, the sole subject of his preaching (Barnes, Robertson); presenting himself not as lord over their faith but as their "servant for Jesus' sake," devoted to their good for Christ's sake (Barnes, Calvin). Cyril notes the confession of Christ's lordship, his deity and sovereignty. The verse displays Paul preaching Christ as Lord. The verse marks the subject: not ourselves, but Christ. The verse displays the lordship: Christ the Lord. The verse displays the humility: ourselves your servants. The verse displays the motive: for Jesus' sake. The preaching displays Christ exalted as Lord, the minister as servant. The verse displays the proclamation: of Christ. The verse displays the lordship: supreme. The verse displays the service: to the church. That Paul preaches Christ as Lord, and himself as servant, displays the true subject and spirit of the ministry: he proclaims not himself but Christ Jesus the Lord, and offers himself as the church's servant for Jesus' sake — Christ exalted, the minister humbled. The verse displays the Christ-centred, servant ministry, grounded in the God who shines in the heart (v.6).
6:
"For God, who commanded the light to shine out of darkness, hath shined in our hearts, to give the light of the knowledge of the glory of God in the face of Jesus Christ." The witnesses note the inward shining of God's light in the knowledge of his glory in Christ. On "God, who commanded the light to shine out of darkness," the grammatical voices note the creation parallel. Barnes and Robertson note Paul alludes to creation (Genesis 1:3): the same God who, at creation, commanded light to shine out of darkness, now works a new creation in the soul.

On "hath shined in our hearts," the witnesses note the inward illumination. Barnes and Calvin note that God "hath shined in our hearts" — a divine, inward illumination, dispelling the darkness of the unregenerate mind, a new creation of light within.

On "the light of the knowledge of the glory of God in the face of Jesus Christ," the witnesses note the object. Barnes and Cyril note the light God gives is "the knowledge of the glory of God" — seen "in the face (person) of Jesus Christ," in whom God's glory is revealed; to know Christ is to see the glory of God. The verse displays the inward shining of God's light in the knowledge of his glory in Christ.

The witnesses agree: Paul alludes to creation (Genesis 1:3) — the same God who commanded light out of darkness now "hath shined in our hearts," a divine inward illumination dispelling the darkness of the unregenerate mind, a new creation of light (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin); the light being "the knowledge of the glory of God," seen "in the face (person) of Jesus Christ," in whom God's glory is revealed (Barnes, Cyril). The verse displays the inward shining. The verse marks the creation: light out of darkness. The verse displays the illumination: in our hearts. The verse displays the object: the glory of God. The verse displays the place: the face of Christ. The shining displays God's new-creation light revealing his glory in Christ. The verse displays the command: light to shine. The verse displays the heart: illumined. The verse displays the Christ: the glory of God. The inward shining of God's light in the knowledge of his glory in Christ displays the new creation God works in the soul: the God who made light at creation shines in the believer's heart, giving the saving knowledge of his own glory as it is revealed in the face of Jesus Christ. The verse displays the divine illumination of the heart, held in earthen vessels (v.7).
7:
"But we have this treasure in earthen vessels, that the excellency of the power may be of God, and not of us." The witnesses note the treasure of the gospel in earthen vessels. On "this treasure in earthen vessels," the grammatical voices note the contrast. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's striking figure: "this treasure" (the glorious gospel, the light of the knowledge of God) is held "in earthen vessels" (fragile, common clay pots) — the apostles, weak, mortal, afflicted men; the precious treasure in frail containers.

On "that the excellency of the power may be of God, and not of us," the witnesses note the design. Barnes and Calvin note God's purpose in this: that the surpassing "power" (the converting, sustaining power evident in the ministry) might manifestly be "of God, and not of us" — the weakness of the vessels making clear that the power belongs to God alone.

The eastern Cyril and Reformed Calvin note the wisdom: God deliberately entrusts the treasure to weak men, that all the glory of its effects may be ascribed to him, not to the human instruments. The verse displays the treasure of the gospel in earthen vessels.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's striking figure — "this treasure" (the glorious gospel, the light of the knowledge of God) is held "in earthen vessels" (fragile clay pots), the apostles being weak, mortal, afflicted men (Barnes, Robertson); God's design being that the surpassing "power" might manifestly be "of God, and not of us," the weakness of the vessels making clear the power is God's alone (Barnes, Calvin). Cyril and Calvin note God deliberately entrusts the treasure to weak men, that all glory be ascribed to him. The verse displays the treasure in earthen vessels. The verse marks the treasure: the gospel. The verse displays the vessels: earthen, frail. The verse displays the design: the power of God. The verse displays the wisdom: glory to God alone. The treasure displays the gospel's power shown the more in weak vessels. The verse displays the treasure: precious. The verse displays the vessel: fragile. The verse displays the power: God's. The treasure of the gospel in earthen vessels displays God's wise design: the precious treasure of the gospel is held in the frail clay of weak, mortal men, that the surpassing power evident in the ministry may manifestly belong to God and not to the human instruments. The verse displays the divine power shining through human weakness, illustrated in the apostles' afflictions (vv.8-12).
8:
"We are troubled on every side, yet not distressed; we are perplexed, but not in despair;" The witnesses note the apostles afflicted yet not overcome. On "troubled on every side, yet not distressed," the grammatical voices note the first paradox. Barnes and Robertson note Paul describes the apostles' sufferings in a series of contrasts: "troubled (pressed, afflicted) on every side, yet not distressed (not crushed, not straitened)" — hard pressed but not hemmed in to destruction.

On "perplexed, but not in despair," the witnesses note the second paradox. Barnes and Chrysostom note "perplexed" (at a loss, not knowing what to do) "but not in despair" (not utterly without resource or hope) — a wordplay: at a loss but not at a total loss; bewildered but not despairing.

The Reformed Calvin and puritan Henry note the point: these paradoxes show the divine power sustaining the frail vessels — afflicted, yet upheld; the power of God in v.7 displayed in their preservation amid suffering. The verse displays the apostles afflicted yet not overcome.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul describes the apostles' sufferings in contrasts — "troubled (pressed, afflicted) on every side, yet not distressed (not crushed)," hard pressed but not hemmed in to destruction (Barnes, Robertson); "perplexed (at a loss) but not in despair (not without resource)," a wordplay: at a loss but not at a total loss (Barnes, Chrysostom). Calvin and Henry note these paradoxes show the divine power sustaining the frail vessels — afflicted yet upheld. The verse displays the apostles afflicted yet not overcome. The verse marks the trouble: on every side. The verse displays the preservation: not distressed. The verse displays the perplexity: not despair. The verse displays the power: God sustaining. The paradoxes display the divine power upholding the afflicted apostles. The verse displays the affliction: pressing. The verse displays the deliverance: not crushed. The verse displays the hope: not despairing. The apostles afflicted yet not overcome display the power of God sustaining the earthen vessels: hard pressed on every side yet not crushed, perplexed yet not despairing — their preservation amid suffering proving that the power is God's. The verse displays the divine upholding in affliction, continued in persecution and casting down (v.9).
9:
"Persecuted, but not forsaken; cast down, but not destroyed;" The brief verse notes the apostles persecuted yet not forsaken or destroyed. The grammatical voices note the further paradoxes. Barnes and Robertson note Paul continues the contrasts: "persecuted, but not forsaken" (pursued by enemies, yet not abandoned by God); "cast down, but not destroyed" (struck down, thrown to the ground, yet not killed or made an end of).

On the imagery, the witnesses note the figure. Barnes notes the language may be drawn from the battlefield or the arena — pursued, struck down, but not slain; God's preserving hand evident in each deliverance.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the comfort: in every extremity the apostles are upheld; God does not forsake them nor let them be destroyed — the power of God sustaining them through all. The verse displays the apostles persecuted yet not forsaken or destroyed.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul continues the contrasts — "persecuted, but not forsaken" (pursued by enemies, yet not abandoned by God); "cast down, but not destroyed" (struck down, yet not killed, Barnes, Robertson); the imagery perhaps from the battlefield or arena — pursued, struck down, but not slain, God's preserving hand in each deliverance (Barnes). Wesley notes that in every extremity the apostles are upheld, God not forsaking them. The verse displays the apostles persecuted yet not forsaken. The verse marks the persecution: not forsaken. The verse displays the casting down: not destroyed. The verse displays the imagery: battlefield or arena. The verse displays the comfort: God upholds. The paradoxes display God's preserving hand in every extremity. The verse displays the pursuit: by enemies. The verse displays the deliverance: not abandoned. The verse displays the survival: not slain. The apostles persecuted yet not forsaken or destroyed display God's preserving power in every extremity: pursued by enemies yet not abandoned by God, struck down yet not killed — in each peril the divine hand upholds and delivers them. The verse displays the unfailing preservation of God's servants, bearing the dying of Jesus (vv.10-12). In each pair the first word concedes the full reality of the peril while the second quietly denies it the last word, so that the whole series becomes a catalogue not of the apostle’s endurance but of the unfailing power of God that bore him through every extremity unbroken.
10:
"Always bearing about in the body the dying of the Lord Jesus, that the life also of Jesus might be made manifest in our body." The witnesses note the apostles bearing the dying of Jesus, that his life be manifested. On "bearing about in the body the dying of the Lord Jesus," the grammatical voices note the fellowship in suffering. Barnes and Robertson note Paul and the apostles "always" carry about "the dying (the death-process, the constant exposure to death) of the Lord Jesus" — in their bodies they share Christ's sufferings, bearing in themselves the marks and likeness of his death.

On "that the life also of Jesus might be made manifest in our body," the witnesses note the purpose. Barnes and Calvin note the design: that "the life of Jesus" (his risen, sustaining life, his power) might be displayed in their bodies — their preservation amid deadly perils manifesting the living power of the risen Christ.

The Reformed Calvin notes the pattern: as the apostles share Christ's death in their sufferings, so they share his life in their deliverances; the dying and the rising of Jesus are both displayed in them. The verse displays the apostles bearing the dying of Jesus, that his life be manifested.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul and the apostles "always" carry about "the dying of the Lord Jesus" — sharing Christ's sufferings, bearing in their bodies the likeness of his death (Barnes, Robertson); the design being that "the life of Jesus" (his risen, sustaining power) might be displayed in their bodies, their preservation amid deadly perils manifesting the living power of the risen Christ (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin notes that as they share Christ's death in suffering, so they share his life in deliverance. The verse displays the apostles bearing Christ's dying. The verse marks the bearing: the dying of Jesus. The verse displays the purpose: the life of Jesus manifested. The verse displays the fellowship: in his death. The verse displays the power: of his risen life. The bearing displays Christ's death and life both shown in the apostles. The verse displays the dying: borne. The verse displays the life: manifested. The verse displays the pattern: death and resurrection. The apostles bearing the dying of Jesus, that his life be manifested, display the pattern of Christ's death and resurrection in his servants: in their constant exposure to death they share Christ's sufferings, that the living power of the risen Christ might be displayed in their preservation. The verse displays the fellowship of Christ's death and life in the ministry, applied to their daily peril (vv.11-12).
11:
"For we which live are alway delivered unto death for Jesus' sake, that the life also of Jesus might be made manifest in our mortal flesh." The brief verse notes the apostles delivered to death for Jesus' sake, that his life be shown. The grammatical voices note the restatement. Barnes and Robertson note Paul repeats and explains v.10: "we which live" (the living apostles) are "alway delivered unto death for Jesus' sake" — constantly exposed to deadly peril because of their service to Christ.

On "that the life of Jesus might be made manifest in our mortal flesh," the witnesses note the purpose. Barnes notes the same design: that the life (the sustaining power) of Jesus might be displayed "in our mortal flesh" — the more clearly because the flesh is mortal and frail; the survival of dying men manifests the power of the living Christ.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the paradox: the very mortality of their flesh, kept alive amid constant danger, displays the immortal life and power of Jesus. The verse displays the apostles delivered to death, that Christ's life be manifested.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul repeats and explains v.10 — "we which live" are "alway delivered unto death for Jesus' sake," constantly exposed to deadly peril for his service (Barnes, Robertson); the design being that the life (sustaining power) of Jesus might be displayed "in our mortal flesh," the more clearly because the flesh is frail, the survival of dying men manifesting the power of the living Christ (Barnes). Wesley notes the very mortality of their flesh, kept alive amid danger, displays the immortal life of Jesus. The verse displays the apostles delivered to death. The verse marks the peril: delivered unto death. The verse displays the cause: for Jesus' sake. The verse displays the purpose: the life of Jesus manifested. The verse displays the paradox: in mortal flesh. The deliverance displays Christ's life shown in mortal, dying men. The verse displays the death: constant. The verse displays the life: of Jesus. The verse displays the flesh: mortal. The apostles delivered to death for Jesus' sake, that his life be shown, display the paradox of the ministry: the living apostles are continually given over to deadly peril for Christ, that the sustaining life of Jesus might shine the more clearly in their frail, mortal flesh. The verse displays the power of Christ's life in dying men, with its fruit for the church (v.12).
12:
"So then death worketh in us, but life in you." The brief verse notes that death works in the apostles, life in the church. The grammatical voices note the result. Barnes and Robertson note Paul draws the conclusion: "so then death worketh in us" (the apostles' sufferings and exposure to death) "but life in you" (the Corinthians) — the apostles' deadly toils and dangers result in the spiritual life and benefit of the church.

On the exchange, the witnesses note the fruitfulness. Barnes notes the gracious exchange: the apostles endure dying, and the church gains life; their sufferings are the means by which the gospel, and life through it, comes to the believers.

The Reformed Calvin notes the comfort: the apostles' afflictions are not in vain but fruitful — through their dying, life flows to others; a pattern of self-sacrificing ministry for the good of the church. The verse displays that death works in the apostles, life in the church.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul concludes — "death worketh in us" (the apostles' sufferings and exposure to death) "but life in you" (the church), the apostles' deadly toils resulting in the spiritual life of the believers (Barnes, Robertson); the gracious exchange by which the apostles endure dying and the church gains life through the gospel they bring (Barnes). Calvin notes the apostles' afflictions are fruitful, life flowing to others through their dying. The verse displays death in the apostles, life in the church. The verse marks the death: in the apostles. The verse displays the life: in the church. The verse displays the exchange: dying for others' life. The verse displays the fruitfulness: of suffering. The conclusion displays the apostles' dying yielding life to the church. The verse displays the toil: of the apostles. The verse displays the benefit: to the church. The verse displays the pattern: self-sacrificing ministry. That death works in the apostles, life in the church, displays the fruitful exchange of self-sacrificing ministry: the apostles' sufferings and exposure to death are the very means by which spiritual life flows to the believers — their dying yields the church's life. The verse displays the costly fruitfulness of the ministry, sustained by the same spirit of faith (vv.13-15).
13:
"We having the same spirit of faith, according as it is written, I believed, and therefore have I spoken; we also believe, and therefore speak;" The witnesses note the spirit of faith that believes and therefore speaks. On "the same spirit of faith," the grammatical voices note the shared faith. Barnes and Robertson note Paul claims "the same spirit of faith" as the psalmist — the same believing disposition (wrought by the Spirit) that animated the saints of old.

On "I believed, and therefore have I spoken," the witnesses note the citation. Barnes and Henry note Paul quotes Psalm 116:10: faith in the heart issues in confession with the mouth — "I believed, therefore have I spoken"; the psalmist, amid affliction, believed and so spoke.

On "we also believe, and therefore speak," the Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the application: like the psalmist, the apostles, sustained by faith amid their sufferings, believe and therefore continue to preach — their faith (in the resurrection, v.14) the spring of their bold testimony. The verse displays the spirit of faith that believes and therefore speaks.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul claims "the same spirit of faith" as the psalmist — the same believing disposition wrought by the Spirit (Barnes, Robertson); quoting Psalm 116:10, "I believed, therefore have I spoken," faith in the heart issuing in confession (Barnes, Henry); so the apostles, sustained by faith amid sufferings, "believe and therefore speak," their faith the spring of their bold testimony (Calvin, Wesley). The verse displays the spirit of faith. The verse marks the faith: the same spirit. The verse displays the citation: Psalm 116. The verse displays the issue: believing and speaking. The verse displays the spring: faith. The faith displays believing issuing in bold testimony. The verse displays the believing: of heart. The verse displays the speaking: of mouth. The verse displays the apostles: like the psalmist. The spirit of faith that believes and therefore speaks displays the spring of the apostles' bold testimony: sharing the psalmist's believing spirit ("I believed, therefore have I spoken"), they too believe and therefore preach — their faith, even amid suffering, issuing in confession. The verse displays the faith that animates the ministry, grounded in the hope of resurrection (v.14).
14:
"Knowing that he which raised up the Lord Jesus shall raise up us also by Jesus, and shall present us with you." The brief verse notes the hope of resurrection through Jesus. The grammatical voices note the assurance. Barnes and Ellicott note the ground of the apostles' faith and boldness: "knowing" (with assured confidence) that God, "who raised up the Lord Jesus, shall raise up us also" — the resurrection of Christ the pledge of the believer's resurrection.

On "by Jesus, and shall present us with you," the witnesses note the manner and goal. Barnes notes the resurrection is "by (through) Jesus" — by his power and as the firstfruits — and that God "shall present us with you" — bringing the apostles and the Corinthians together into his presence, glorified, at the last day.

The grammatical-historical note the comfort: this certain hope of resurrection sustains the apostles amid their dying; death is not the end, for the same God who raised Jesus will raise them. The verse displays the hope of resurrection through Jesus.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the ground of the apostles' boldness is "knowing" (with assured confidence) that God, who raised the Lord Jesus, shall raise them also — Christ's resurrection the pledge of the believer's (Barnes, Ellicott); the resurrection "by (through) Jesus," by his power, and God "shall present us with you," bringing apostles and Corinthians together glorified into his presence (Barnes). The grammatical-historical note this hope sustains the apostles amid their dying. The verse displays the hope of resurrection. The verse marks the assurance: knowing. The verse displays the pledge: he raised Jesus. The verse displays the resurrection: us also. The verse displays the goal: presented together. The hope displays the certain resurrection sustaining the apostles. The verse displays the raising: of Jesus. The verse displays the raising: of us. The verse displays the presentation: glorified. The hope of resurrection through Jesus displays the assured ground of the apostles' faith amid death: knowing that the God who raised the Lord Jesus will raise them also by him and present them with the believers glorified, they face their dying without dismay. The verse displays the resurrection hope that sustains the ministry, ordered to God's glory (v.15).
15:
"For all things are for your sakes, that the abundant grace might through the thanksgiving of many redound to the glory of God." The brief verse notes that all is for the church's sake and God's glory. The grammatical voices note the design. Barnes and Robertson note Paul affirms that "all things" — his sufferings, labours, and the whole ministry — are "for your sakes" (for the benefit of the Corinthians and the church), not for himself.

On "that the abundant grace... redound to the glory of God," the witnesses note the ultimate end. Barnes and Calvin note the goal: that the "abundant grace" (the grace spreading to more and more people through the ministry) might, "through the thanksgiving of many," "redound (overflow, abound) to the glory of God" — the more are saved, the more thanksgiving rises, and the more God is glorified.

The Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the chain: the apostles suffer for the church's good, the church's salvation produces thanksgiving, and thanksgiving glorifies God — the whole ministry ordered to God's glory. The verse displays that all is for the church's sake and God's glory.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: "all things" — Paul's sufferings, labours, the whole ministry — are "for your sakes," for the church's benefit, not himself (Barnes, Robertson); the goal being that the "abundant grace" (spreading to more and more) might, "through the thanksgiving of many," redound to the glory of God — the more saved, the more thanksgiving, the more God glorified (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin and Wesley note the chain: suffering for the church, salvation producing thanksgiving, thanksgiving glorifying God. The verse displays all for the church and God's glory. The verse marks the design: for your sakes. The verse displays the grace: abundant, spreading. The verse displays the thanksgiving: of many. The verse displays the end: God's glory. The design displays the ministry ordered to grace, thanksgiving, and glory. The verse displays the all-things: for the church. The verse displays the grace: overflowing. The verse displays the glory: of God. That all is for the church's sake and God's glory displays the ultimate design of the ministry: the apostles' sufferings and labours are for the church's benefit, that the spreading grace, through the thanksgiving of the many who are saved, might overflow to the glory of God. The verse displays the God-glorifying end of all the apostle's toil, encouraging him not to faint (v.16).
16:
"For which cause we faint not; but though our outward man perish, yet the inward man is renewed day by day." The witnesses note the inward man renewed though the outward perishes. On "we faint not," the grammatical voices note the resolve. Barnes and Robertson note Paul repeats his theme (from v.1): "for which cause" — because of the glory of the ministry and the hope of resurrection — "we faint not," do not lose heart amid all the sufferings.

On "though our outward man perish," the witnesses note the decay. Barnes and Calvin note "the outward man" (the body, the physical frame) is "perishing" (decaying, wasting away under affliction, age, and toil) — visibly wearing out.

On "the inward man is renewed day by day," the Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the contrast: while the body decays, "the inward man" (the soul, the spiritual life, the new nature) "is renewed day by day" — daily strengthened, refreshed, and growing by grace; the believer's spiritual life flourishing as the body fails. The verse displays the inward man renewed though the outward perishes.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul repeats his theme — "for which cause" (the glory of the ministry, the resurrection hope) "we faint not" (Barnes, Robertson); "the outward man" (the body) is "perishing" (decaying under affliction and age, Barnes, Calvin); but "the inward man" (the soul, the new nature) "is renewed day by day," daily strengthened and growing by grace, flourishing as the body fails (Calvin, Wesley). The verse displays the inward man renewed. The verse marks the resolve: we faint not. The verse displays the decay: the outward man perishes. The verse displays the renewal: the inward man renewed. The verse displays the contrast: body failing, soul flourishing. The renewal displays the spiritual life growing as the body decays. The verse displays the outward: perishing. The verse displays the inward: renewed. The verse displays the daily: renewal. The inward man renewed though the outward perishes displays the believer's spiritual flourishing amid bodily decay: while the body wastes away under affliction and age, the soul is daily renewed, strengthened, and refreshed by grace — so the apostle does not faint. The verse displays the daily renewal of the inner life, weighed against the eternal glory (vv.17-18).
17:
"For our light affliction, which is but for a moment, worketh for us a far more exceeding and eternal weight of glory;" The witnesses note that present light affliction works an eternal weight of glory. On "our light affliction, which is but for a moment," the grammatical voices note the contrast. Barnes and Robertson note Paul weighs the present sufferings against the coming glory: the affliction, though severe, is "light" (in comparison) and "but for a moment" (momentary, transient) — small and brief beside what awaits.

On "a far more exceeding and eternal weight of glory," the witnesses note the surpassing recompense. Barnes and Calvin note the contrast at every point: light vs "weight," momentary vs "eternal," affliction vs "glory" — and "far more exceeding" (beyond all measure); the coming glory infinitely outweighs the present suffering.

On "worketh for us," the Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the connection: the affliction "worketh (produces, achieves)" the glory — not as merit, but as the appointed means and preparation; the trials themselves, by God's grace, work toward the eternal reward. The verse displays that present light affliction works an eternal weight of glory.

The witnesses agree: Paul weighs present sufferings against coming glory — the affliction, though severe, is "light" (in comparison) and "but for a moment" (transient), small and brief beside what awaits (Barnes, Robertson); against it stands "a far more exceeding and eternal weight of glory," the contrast at every point (light vs weight, momentary vs eternal, affliction vs glory), the coming glory infinitely outweighing the present (Barnes, Calvin); and the affliction "worketh (produces)" the glory, not as merit but as the appointed means (Calvin, Wesley). The verse displays affliction working glory. The verse marks the affliction: light, momentary. The verse displays the glory: exceeding, eternal weight. The verse displays the contrast: at every point. The verse displays the working: affliction toward glory. The contrast displays the eternal glory infinitely outweighing present suffering. The verse displays the affliction: light. The verse displays the glory: weighty. The verse displays the duration: eternal. That present light affliction works an eternal weight of glory displays the surpassing recompense that sustains the apostle: the momentary, comparatively light suffering is producing, by God's grace, a glory beyond all measure — eternal, weighty, and surpassing — that infinitely outweighs it. The verse displays the eternal glory set against present trial, grounded in looking at the unseen (v.18).
18:
"While we look not at the things which are seen, but at the things which are not seen: for the things which are seen are temporal; but the things which are not seen are eternal." The witnesses note the gaze fixed on the eternal unseen, not the temporal seen. On "we look not at the things which are seen, but at the things which are not seen," the grammatical voices note the gaze. Barnes and Robertson note the condition of the foregoing (v.17): the affliction works glory "while we look not (do not fix our gaze) at the things seen (the visible, present, earthly) but at the things not seen (the invisible, future, heavenly)" — the eye of faith fixed on the unseen.

On "the things seen are temporal; the things not seen are eternal," the witnesses note the reason. Barnes and Calvin note the ground: the seen things are "temporal" (temporary, passing, lasting only for a time) while the unseen are "eternal" (everlasting, abiding forever) — so the eternal is infinitely more worthy of regard.

The Reformed Calvin notes the principle: faith looks beyond the visible and transient to the invisible and eternal; this heavenly perspective makes the present afflictions light and the coming glory weighty. The verse displays the gaze fixed on the eternal unseen.

The witnesses agree: the affliction works glory "while we look not (do not fix our gaze) at the things seen (visible, present, earthly) but at the things not seen (invisible, future, heavenly)" — the eye of faith fixed on the unseen (Barnes, Robertson); the ground being that the seen are "temporal" (passing, lasting only a time) and the unseen "eternal" (abiding forever), so the eternal is infinitely more worthy of regard (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin notes faith looks beyond the visible to the eternal, this perspective making affliction light and glory weighty. The verse displays the gaze on the unseen. The verse marks the gaze: not the seen, but the unseen. The verse displays the reason: temporal vs eternal. The verse displays the faith: fixed on the invisible. The verse displays the perspective: heavenly. The gaze displays faith fixed on the eternal unseen. The verse displays the seen: temporal. The verse displays the unseen: eternal. The verse displays the eye: of faith. The gaze fixed on the eternal unseen, not the temporal seen, displays the heavenly perspective that sustains the apostle: the eye of faith looks past the visible and passing to the invisible and everlasting — and because the seen are temporal and the unseen eternal, the present afflictions seem light and the coming glory weighty. The verse displays the faith-perspective that crowns the chapter on the ministry sustained amid suffering by eternal hope.
